_comment: Hand-curated index of external BBS-history archives the guru can point users at.  Loaded by llm_tools/external_archives.js's external_archives() tool.  Each entry is matched substring-style against the user's query (tags + summary + url).  Keep it small (~50 entries) -- this is a tool, not a RAG corpus.  When adding entries, prefer canonical landing pages over deep links that may rot.

textfiles.blurb: Massive archive of text files from the BBS era, organized by topic.  Plus sister sites for ANSI art, CD-ROMs, PDFs, audio, and old web content.

bbsdoc.blurb: Eight-episode documentary on the history of bulletin board systems, released 2005.  Long-form interviews with sysops, software authors, and scene participants.

entries.summary: textfiles.com's BBS section -- history articles, sysop newsletters, phone-number lists, BBS software docs, and the culture that surrounded bulletin boards.  The single best landing page for BBS-era research.
Jason Scott's curated "100 files" collection -- the text files he considers the most important / influential from the BBS era.  Good first stop for newcomers.
Archive of BBS-era newsletters and online magazines -- the sysop press that covered the scene as it happened.
Sysop-specific guides, how-tos, ethics docs, and tools -- written by and for the people who actually ran BBSes.
Online magazines from the BBS era -- Phrack, 2600, Cult of the Dead Cow, NIA, LOD/H, and many more.  The BBS scene's own self-documentation.
Captured BBS message-base threads and echo-mail conversations -- a snapshot of what people actually talked about on BBSes.
Catalogs and NFOs from warez / pirate / scene groups -- the social organizations that drove much of the BBS file-trading economy.
Files about software piracy -- philosophy, methodology, and the back-and-forth between crackers and copy protection.
BBS-era hacking text files -- system penetration, security holes, exploits, and the underground that documented them.
Phone phreaking text files -- blue/red/black box plans, telco internals, COCOTs, and the long history of phone-system hacking that predated the BBS.
Anarchy files -- the Anarchist Cookbook era, lockpicking, and the genre of "forbidden knowledge" text files that defined a chunk of BBS culture (rightly or wrongly).
Humor text files -- jokes, parodies, fake news, and the long-running comedy genres of the BBS era.
Game-related text files -- walkthroughs, hints, FAQs, and door-game docs (TradeWars 2002, LORD, etc.).
Programming text files -- BBS software source, tutorials, language references, and the docs that taught a generation to code on dial-up.
Early-internet text files -- the BBS-to-internet transition, TCP/IP primers, UUCP/Usenet docs, the world before the web.
Computer-specific archives -- Apple II, Atari, C64, Amiga, IBM PC, Tandy/TRS-80, and others.  Per-platform docs, tips, and software.
Fiction text files -- short stories, novellas, and the creative-writing scene that lived on BBSes.
Conspiracy text files -- JFK, Roswell-adjacent, secret societies, and the conspiratorial fringe that found a home on BBSes.
Occult and esoteric text files -- another fringe that BBSes hosted before the web.
Recently uploaded files to textfiles.com -- the still-growing edge of an otherwise historical archive.
The ANSI/ASCII art archive (artscene.textfiles.com) -- artpacks from ACiD, iCE, FiRE, etc., plus the history of the art scene that produced BBS login screens.
Shareware CD-ROM archive (cd.textfiles.com) -- ISOs and contents of classic shareware compilations (Night Owl, Walnut Creek, etc.).
PDF archive (pdf.textfiles.com) -- scanned manuals, magazines, and documents that don't fit the plain-text mold.
Old-web archive (web.textfiles.com) -- captured early websites, GeoCities-era pages, and the immediate post-BBS web.
Audio archive (audio.textfiles.com) -- voicemail captures, phone-system recordings, and BBS-era audio artifacts.

Jason Scott's 2005 documentary film about the bulletin board system era -- eight episodes, 5+ hours of interviews with sysops, authors, and scene participants.  Released under a Creative Commons license.

The BBS Documentary Library -- Jason Scott's curated commentary on key BBS-era topics: famous incidents, lawsuits, services, door games, graphics protocols, controversies, and etymology.  Where to read about LORD, TradeWars, PC-Pursuit, the Steve Jackson Games raid, SEA vs. PKWARE, the modem tax rumor, and more.

Library entry on Legend of the Red Dragon (LORD) -- Seth Robinson's 1989 fantasy door game, ported to DOS / Worldgroup / Wildcat / etc., later maintained by Metropolis Gameport with Michael Preslar as lead programmer.  One of the iconic BBS door games.
Library entry on TradeWars -- the space-trading multi-player door game that became one of the most-played and most-modified BBS games of all time.
Library entry on Dope Wars -- John E. Dell's 1984 economic-simulation door game (originally a high-school project), one of the most ported and revisioned BBS games of the era.
Library entry on Barren Realms Elite (BRE) -- a strategy door game popular on the InterBBS leagues that linked competing BBSes together for inter-system games.
Library entry on Pimp Wars -- Paul J. Martino's 1990 door game, long-supported by the author and a cultural-touchstone (if dated) BBS title.
Library entry on ANSI -- the escape-code graphics standard that became the BBS world's lingua franca for color, cursor positioning, and the art that defined login screens.
Library entry on RIPscript -- TeleGrafix's vector-graphics protocol that briefly promised GUIs over BBS connections in the mid-90s, just before the web rendered it moot.
Library entry on NAPLPS -- the North American Presentation Level Protocol, a pre-ANSI/RIP attempt at standardized BBS graphics, used by Prodigy and some commercial BBSes.
Library entry on PC-Pursuit -- Sprint's packet-network service that let BBS users dial cross-country boards at flat rates, one of the unsung heroes of the long-distance-BBS era.

Library entry on SEA vs. PKWARE -- the late-1980s lawsuit between System Enhancement Associates (ARC) and Phil Katz / PKWARE (PKARC, later PKZIP), one of the biggest controversies the BBS world ever saw.
Library entry on the Steve Jackson Games raid -- the 1990 Secret Service seizure of SJG's Illuminati BBS, a landmark incident that helped catalyze the founding of the Electronic Frontier Foundation.
Library entry on the Modem Tax rumors -- the recurring 1980s/90s urban legend that the FCC was about to tax modem-using phone lines, sparking panic chain letters across BBSes for years.

Library entry on FOSSIL -- the Fido Opus Seadog Standard Interface Layer serial driver spec that abstracted COM-port access for DOS BBS software (X00, BNU, etc.).  Essential infrastructure for the FidoNet-era BBS.
Library entry on "warez" -- the etymology and history of the term as it emerged from BBS pirate culture.

Library entry on the WWIVwar -- the early-90s split between WWIVnet (run by WWIV author Wayne Bell) and the breakaway WWIVlink network, a cautionary tale about how minor disagreements can fracture online communities.

The BBS Software Directory -- Jason Scott's canonical catalog of every known BBS software package across every platform (CP/M, Apple, Atari, Commodore, IBM DOS/OS2/Windows, Tandy, TI-99, Unix, VAX/VMS, etc.).  ~900 entries with version histories, authors, and screenshots.

Software directory entry for Synchronet -- Rob Swindell's multi-line BBS, originally released as commercial DOS software (Digital Dynamics, 1991) and now open-source / cross-platform (DOS, OS/2, Windows, Linux, BSD, macOS).  This is the software the host BBS runs.
Software directory entry for PCBoard -- Fred Clark / Clark Development's commercial DOS BBS, one of the dominant 1990s packages, known for PPE (PCBoard Programming Language) extensibility.
Software directory entry for WWIV -- Wayne Bell's BBS, source-licensed (a rare thing in the 1990s) and the basis for WWIVnet plus an enormous modding community.
Software directory entry for Wildcat! -- Mustang Software's commercial DOS BBS, known for its menu-driven sysop UI (vs. config-file-heavy competitors).
Software directory entry for Renegade -- Cott Miller's DOS BBS, a Telegard fork that grew into its own thing and built a deep enthusiast community.
Software directory entry for RemoteAccess (RA) -- Andrew Milner / Wantree Development's commercial DOS BBS, hugely popular in Australia, Europe, and the FidoNet world.
Software directory entry for Telegard -- Tim Strike's DOS BBS, ancestor of Renegade and a major name in the late-80s / early-90s ANSI-art scene.
Software directory entry for TBBS (The Bread Board System) -- Phil Becker / eSoft's commercial multi-line BBS, one of the earliest to support many simultaneous callers on a single PC.
Software directory entry for Spitfire -- Buffalo Creek Software's shareware DOS BBS, an inexpensive option that filled the gap between freeware and PCBoard-class systems.
Software directory entry for Maximus -- Scott Dudley / Lanius Corporation's DOS (later OS/2 and Unix) BBS, a FidoNet powerhouse with a strong technical reputation.

Software directory entry for Mystic BBS -- a modern multi-platform BBS by g4mystic, with its own scripting language (MPL).  Still actively developed; one of today's most common Synchronet alternatives.
Software directory entry for The Major BBS -- Tim Stryker / Galacticomm's high-end commercial BBS (later renamed Worldgroup), known for native MUD-style multi-user games and 256+ simultaneous lines.
Software directory entry for Searchlight -- Frank LaPinske's DOS BBS, an early adopter of RIP graphics support and a regional favorite.
Software directory entry for Opus-CBCS -- Wynn Wagner's freeware DOS BBS, the first BBS software with native FidoNet support and an enormous influence on the network's growth.
Software directory entry for RBBS-PC -- Tom Mack's free, BASIC-source DOS BBS, one of the most-deployed early BBS programs because the source was open and modifiable.

Software directory entry for the original Citadel -- Jeff Prothero / Cynbe ru Taren's CP/M BBS that invented the "room" message-base model and spawned dozens of forks across every platform.  See also http://anticlimactic.org/Citadel/ for the lineage.
Software directory entry for CBBS -- Ward Christensen and Randy Suess's 1978 S-100 kit-computer system, the first computerized bulletin board.  Patient zero for the entire BBS phenomenon.
Software directory entry for Virtual BBS (VBBS) -- a DOS BBS that grew its own VirtualNET inter-BBS network, popular in the early 90s.
Software directory entry for PowerBBS -- Russell Frey's shareware DOS (later Windows) BBS, popular in budget-conscious sysop circles.
Software directory entry for Ezycom -- Peter Davies's DOS BBS, known for being well-documented and easy to set up, with a strong FidoNet feature set.

The TEXTFILES.COM BBS Timeline -- a chronological list of 261 BBS-related events from 1874 to 2002 (telegraph and telephone precursors through the end of the dial-up era).  Best resource for "when did X happen" style questions.

Anticlimactic.org's Citadel archive -- a comprehensive collection of Citadel-family software, lore, and the lineage tree connecting every fork (referenced by the BBS Software Directory itself as the canonical Citadel source).
BBS Documentary Episode 1: "BAUD" -- the origins, Ward Christensen and Randy Suess inventing CBBS in 1978, and the first wave of homebrew bulletin board systems.
BBS Documentary Episode 2: "SYSOPS AND USERS" -- the social compact between BBS operators and their callers, and the culture that emerged.
BBS Documentary Episode 3: "MAKE IT PAY!" -- the commercial BBS business -- subscription services, The WELL, GEnie, and the on-ramp toward online services.
BBS Documentary Episode 4: "FIDONET" -- Tom Jennings and the creation of FidoNet, the store-and-forward network that connected BBSes worldwide before the internet did.
BBS Documentary Episode 5: "ARTSCENE" -- the ANSI art scene, the art groups (ACiD, iCE) that defined BBS visual identity, and the artists behind login screens.
BBS Documentary Episode 6: "HPAC" -- Hacking, Phreaking, Anarchy, and Cracking.  The underground side of the BBS world and the boards that hosted it.
BBS Documentary Episode 7: "NO COPY" -- the warez scene, software piracy as social practice, and the cracking groups that drove BBS file traffic.
BBS Documentary Episode 8: "COMPRESSION" -- the decline of the BBS as the internet arrived, and reflections from sysops on what was lost and gained.
